articles.author: Sara Aboubakr
articles.description: U.S. President Donald Trump called for Iran's "unconditional surrender" Friday as Israeli warplanes pounded Lebanon and the Islamic Republic, and oil prices rise.
articles.title: Live updates: Israel hammers Beirut and Tehran as Iran attacks more Israeli targets

articles.author: Aamer Madhani, Seung Min Kim
articles.description: President Donald Trump says it's inconsequential if Russia has provided Iran with information to help Tehran target U.S. military personnel and assets in the Middle East. He dismissed the import of such information-sharing on Saturday after attending the dignified transfer for six Army reservists who were killed in a drone strike in Kuwait during the Iran war. Trump stopped short of confirming reports by The Associated Press and other news outlets that U.S. intelligence officials believe Russia has provided Iran with such targeting information. But he said that if Moscow is passing on such information, Iran is getting little out of it.
articles.title: Trump downplays importance of reports Russia is sharing intel with Iran

articles.author: Jon Gambrell, Sam Mednick, Samy Magdy
articles.description: Iran’s president says that a demand by the United States for an unconditional surrender is a “dream that they should take to their grave.” President Masoud Pezeshkian made the statement in a prerecorded address aired by state television. He also apologized for Iran’s attacks on regional countries, saying that Tehran would halt them and suggesting they were caused by miscommunication in the ranks. His statement aired after repeated attacks Saturday morning on Bahrain, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates. He blamed the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and other top leaders for what sounded like a loss of command and control in the armed forces in recent days.
articles.title: Iran's president defies US demands while apologizing for strikes on neighbors

articles.author: Melissa Goldin
articles.description: A deluge of misrepresented or fabricated videos has spread widely online since the Iran war began last weekend, fueled in part by state-linked propaganda influence campaigns — particularly around who is winning the war and how bad casualties have been. Artificial intelligence has helped fuel misinformation in ways that weren’t possible during past conflicts, even just a few years ago. Coupled with state-linked disinformation and censorship, this creates an even wider vacuum in which the truth can get lost.
articles.title: Visual misinformation about Iran war fueled by state actors

articles.author: Wyatte Grantham-Philips, Cathy Bussewitz
articles.description: Oil prices have soared in the week since the U.S. and Israel launched their war against Iran, but President Donald Trump on Saturday downplayed the idea of turning to America’s Strategic Petroleum Reserve to ease the pressure. In the U.S., consumers are already facing higher gas prices, a key cost of living. Trump’s Republican Party is under pressure over the issue of affordability ahead of November midterm elections. Tapping the reserve is among the few things a president can do on his own to try to make an impact on oil prices.
articles.title: Trump is downplaying the need to tap the Strategic Petroleum Reserve

articles.author: Seung Min Kim, Julia Demaree Nikhinson
articles.description: President Donald Trump joined grieving families at Dover Air Force Base for the dignified transfer of six U.S. Army Reserve soldiers killed in Kuwait. On Saturday, Trump and Vice President JD Vance attended the dignified transfer with their spouses and top administration officials. Trump spoke earlier in Miami, calling the troops heroes. He says their return is heartbreaking and he wants to limit U.S. war deaths. Trump stayed silent during the transfer, as protocol requires. He saluted as flag-draped transfer cases moved from the military aircraft to waiting vehicles. The six U.S. soldiers were killed in a drone strike at a command center.
articles.title: Trump joins families for return of soldiers killed in Middle East

articles.author: Rebecca Santana
articles.description: Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem has lost her job after criticism over a flashy ad campaign tied to President Donald Trump’s deportation push. Lawmakers grilled Noem across two days of hearings about a $220 million TV effort that featured her on horseback near Mount Rushmore. Republican Senator John Kennedy questioned the contracting process and whether Trump approved it. Noem said Trump knew and backed the plan. Trump said he did not know, and he was not thrilled. On Thursday, Trump nominated Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin to replace Noem.
articles.title: The ad campaign that prompted Kristi Noem's firing

articles.author: David Bauder
articles.description: A federal judge has ruled that the Trump administration's actions to dismantle the Voice of America over the past year are illegal. U.S. District Court Judge Royce C. Lamberth said that Kari Lake, President Donald Trump's choice to lead the federal agency overseeing Voice of America, did not have authority under the law to take her actions. He ruled Saturday on a lawsuit filed by three journalists laid off by Lake, the former Arizona broadcaster-turned-politician who has been overseeing the U.S. Agency for Global Media. Lake called it a bogus ruling by an activist judge that will be appealed. The journalists say they feel vindicated.
articles.title: Judge rules Kari Lake's actions to dismantle Voice of America are illegal

articles.author: Nicole Winfield
articles.description: Pope Leo XIV named a veteran Vatican diplomat as his new ambassador to the United States. Archbishop Gabriele Caccia is tasked with managing one of the Holy See’s most important bilateral relationships, which has come under strain over the Trump administration’s war in Iran and immigration crackdown. Caccia is currently the Holy See’s ambassador to the United Nations in New York. He replaces Cardinal Christophe Pierre, who at age 80 is retiring as apostolic nuncio in Washington.
articles.title: Pope Leo XIV appoints Vatican diplomat as new US ambassador

articles.author: Mary Clare Jalonick, Lisa Mascaro
articles.description: Visitors to the Capitol in Washington now have a visible reminder of the siege there on Jan. 6, 2021, and the officers who fought and were injured that day. Workers early Saturday quietly installed a plaque honoring the officers. That came three years after the plaque was required by law to be erected. The plaque was placed on the Senate side of the hallway because the Senate voted unanimously in January to install it after House Speaker Mike Johnson had delayed putting it up. The Washington Post first reported the installation, which was witnessed by a reporter about 4 a.m. Saturday. It's the first official Jan. 6 marker in the Capitol.
articles.title: Jan. 6 plaque honoring police officers is finally installed at the Capitol
